sources.notes: Statement and constraints: n <= 12, total m <= 2000, and each day allows luggage to stay or move to one neighboring airport with capacities.
Official editorial page for Codeforces Round 1021; checked as the contest editorial source, but the fetched English tutorial section only showed loading placeholders.
Luogu solution confirms the max-flow to min-cut reduction, O(4^n n m) naive subset transition, and plug/profile DP optimization.
Luogu solution explicitly describes the layered flow graph for every k and the point-by-point profile DP over source-side/sink-side colors.
Chinese writeup gives the same time-expanded network, max-flow min-cut transformation, subset DP state, and O(n m 2^n) row transition.
Independent writeup cross-checks that transition cost is local between adjacent bits of consecutive layers and can be accumulated bit by bit.

annotation.summary: For every prefix of days, maximize how much lost luggage can survive the inspections while each day's movement capacities allow staying or moving to adjacent airports on a cycle.
annotation.constraints: n <= 12 and total m <= 2000; capacities are large, so the bottleneck is exponential in n but must be nearly linear in m.
annotation.core_idea: Build a time-expanded network. Layer 0 has source capacities s_j; each day adds edges from airport j to previous, same, and next airports with capacities a, b, and c; the answer for prefix k is the maximum flow reaching layer k. By max-flow min-cut, compute the minimum cut instead. A cut is described by which airports in a layer are on the source side, so the naive transition between previous subset T and current subset S costs O(4^n) per day. Because each current bit only interacts with three neighboring previous-layer bits on the cycle, determine the next subset bit by bit and keep a rolling profile containing the not-yet-forgotten boundary bits. This profile/plug-style DP reduces each day to O(n 2^n) states while outputting the answer after every day.
annotation.complexity: O(n m 2^n) time and O(2^n) to O(n 2^n) memory depending on implementation
tricks: Turn the survival maximization into a time-expanded max-flow instance.
Use max-flow min-cut to replace flow augmentation with cut-state DP.
Exploit n <= 12 and the local three-edge dependency between adjacent time layers.
Optimize the O(4^n) subset transition by processing the cycle profile bit by bit.

solution_variants.name: min-cut-profile-dp
solution_variants.summary: Maintain dp over the source-side subset of the current time layer. For one new day, process the target subset one airport at a time; the profile stores enough old/new boundary bits to add exactly the cut edges from source side to sink side. After n positions, compress back to a layer subset and print the minimum cut for the all-sink terminal layer.

solution_variants.name: naive-subset-min-cut-dp
solution_variants.summary: For clarity, one can first write a transition over previous subset T and current subset S, adding the capacities of all edges from T to complement(S). This is O(m 4^n) and is only a derivation step for the optimized profile DP.

tags.evidence: The decisive modeling step is a time-expanded max-flow instance whose value is computed through the equivalent minimum cut.
tags.description: Use the max-flow min-cut theorem or directly model a minimum s-t cut as the central optimization object.
tags.created_reason: The existing graph/flow tag is too coarse for problems where the flow graph is only a modeling step and the actual algorithm is min-cut DP.

tags.evidence: The O(n m 2^n) solution processes each layer bit by bit while storing boundary colors of the partially processed cut.
tags.description: Dynamic programming over a moving boundary/profile, often used when local interactions allow forgetting processed cells or bits.
tags.created_reason: Existing bitmask/state-compression tags do not distinguish moving-boundary profile transitions from ordinary subset DP.

tags.evidence: The layer state is a subset of up to 12 airports, and all transitions operate over bitmask states.
The small airport count is exploited by compressing each layer's source-side/sink-side partition into a bitmask.
The flow model expands the process over days into consecutive layers with edges only between adjacent layers.
